message.content:
## Web Searcher: engines-config + measurement

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Your angle: **engines-config + measurement** — Covers angles 5 and 6: head-to-head local open-source engine/config comparison and objective+subjective methods to verify a 'significant gain' before committing to a fine-tune.
Search query: `Kokoro Piper RHVoice eSpeak-NG TTS speed comparison speaking rate settings plus STOI STMI modulation spectrum intelligibility metric MUSHRA listening test protocol`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Web search results for query: "Kokoro Piper RHVoice eSpeak-NG TTS speaking rate comparison intelligibility high speed"

Links: [{"title":"Thank for this :) · Issue #821 · rhasspy/piper","url":"https://github.com/rhasspy/piper/issues/821"},{"title":"Kokoro TTS Review 2026: Is It Really Better Than ElevenLabs? (Tested 45 Days)","url":"https://reviewnexa.com/kokoro-tts-review/"},{"title":"Kokoro TTS Local Setup (2026): Tiny 82M Open Voice Model | Local AI Master","url":"https://localaimaster.com/blog/kokoro-tts-local-setup"},{"title":"Kokoro TTS: Complete Guide — Voices, Languages, WebGPU Setup, and Quality | OfflineTTS","url":"https://www.offlinetts.com/blog/kokoro-tts-complete-guide/"},{"title":"VoXtream2: Full-stream TTS with dynamic speaking rate control","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.13518"},{"title":"Text-to-Speech Models 2026: TTS Leaderboard, Kokoro, XTTS and Voice Agents | CodeSOTA | CodeSOTA","url":"https://www.codesota.com/text-to-speech"},{"title":"The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9938863/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these TTS systems:

## Key Findings

**Kokoro and Piper Comparison:**
Kokoro relies on espeak-ng for grapheme-to-phoneme conversion, which connects it to one of the tools in your query. In terms of quality trade-offs, the Piper voice is very slightly more robotic and some words are slightly less well pronounced than with Kokoro, though Piper is preferred when you need maximum speed on CPU or Raspberry Pi deployment.

**Speed and Performance:**
Kokoro requires pip install kokoro soundfile plus the espeak-ng system package, and Kokoro-82M is an open-weight text-to-speech model with just 82 million parameters that runs fast even on a CPU.

**Quality Considerations:**
The search results indicate that different TTS systems handle speaking rates and intelligibility differently, though specific comparative data on high-speed intelligibility for these particular models wasn't detailed in the results. The challenge is whether a synthetic voice can be directed with emotion, pace, timbre, pauses, and voice quality without losing intelligibility.

Unfortunately, the search results don't contain detailed comparative data on intelligibility at high speaking rates across all these specific models together.

Links: [{"title":"Speech Intelligibility Prediction using Spectro-Temporal Modulation Analysis - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7978234/"},{"title":"A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639302001346"},{"title":"Speech intelligibility prediction using generalized ESTOI with fine-tuned parameters - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639324000402"},{"title":"[PDF] A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/A-spectro-temporal-modulation-index-(STMI)-for-of-Elhilali-Chi/b300ec2a40aaadc78ede0ef8edb91900846b3db6"},{"title":"Binaural Speech Enhancement Using STOI-Optimal Masks","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2209.15472"},{"title":"End-to-End Multi-Task Denoising for the Joint Optimization of Perceptual   Speech Metrics","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1910.10707"},{"title":"A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for ...","url":"https://engineering.jhu.edu/lcap/data/uploads/pdfs/speechcomm2003_ehilali.pdf"},{"title":"End-to-End Waveform Utterance Enhancement for Direct Evaluation Metrics   Optimization by Fully Convolutional Neural Networks","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.03658"},{"title":"An Evaluation of Intrusive Instrumental Intelligibility Metrics","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1708.06027"}]

Now let me search for information about MUSHRA listening test protocol:

Links: [{"title":"MUSHRA Listening Test Overview","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/mushra-listening-test"},{"title":"French Listening Tests for the Assessment of Intelligibility, Quality, and Identity of Body-Conducted Speech Enhancement","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.04495"},{"title":"Rethinking MUSHRA: Addressing Modern Challenges in Text-to-Speech Evaluation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.12719"},{"title":"MUSHRA-NMR: A Fair TTS Evaluation Protocol","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/mushra-nmr-no-mentioned-reference"},{"title":"Comprehensive evaluation of statistical speech waveform synthesis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1811.06296"},{"title":"Scene-Aware Audio Rendering via Deep Acoustic Analysis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1911.06245"},{"title":"MUSHRA - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MUSHRA"},{"title":"MUSHRA — Grokipedia","url":"https://grokipedia.com/page/MUSHRA"},{"title":"High-Fidelity Generative Audio Compression at 0.275kbps","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.00648"},{"title":"Crowdsourcing MUSHRA Tests in the Age of Generative Speech Technologies:","url":"https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2025/lechler25_interspeech.pdf"}]

Based on my web search results, here's comprehensive information about these speech evaluation topics:

## STOI and STMI: Speech Intelligibility Metrics

STOI is an intrusive intelligibility metric based on the correlation between the spectral envelopes of clean and degraded versions of the speech. The STOI score ranges from 0 to 1, and is expected to be monotonically related to the average intelligibility of various listening tests, with a higher STOI value indicating better speech intelligibility.

The STMI is a measure of speech integrity as viewed by a model of the auditory system. The STMI is an elaboration on the STI in that it incorporates explicitly the joint spectro-temporal dimensions of the speech signal, and is applicable to cases of joint (or inseparable) spectro-temporal distortions that are unsuitable for STI measurements.

STMI integrates the filtered spectrograms across the acoustic frequency axis in order to capture the temporal fluctuation of energy in each modulation channel. Related variants include OSTMI and wSTMI, where wSTMI further improves the performance of OSTMI by optimizing the weights assigned to different spectro-temporal modulation channels to maximize speech intelligibility prediction performance.

## MUSHRA Listening Test Protocol

The MUSHRA (MUltiple Stimuli with Hidden Reference and Anchor) listening test is a controlled perceptual evaluation protocol, originally adopted in ITU-R BS.1534, for quantifying perceived audio quality across multiple systems or conditions.

Raters assess multiple stimuli per page, including an explicit reference (a high-quality speech benchmark), an anchor (a lower-quality version for calibration), and an implicit (hidden) reference to normalize judgments. Each stimulus is rated on a continuous 0-100 scale, discretized into five categories: 100-80 (Excellent), 80-60 (Good), 60-40 (Fair), 40-20 (Poor), and 20-0 (Bad).

Stimuli should be around 10 seconds long, with a maximum of 12 seconds, to minimize listener fatigue and enhance response stability. An assessor's responses are excluded if they rate the hidden reference below 90 in more than 15% of test items.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "blind screen reader users fast speech rate Eloquence DECtalk synthesizer preference intelligibility syllables per second"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) On the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech for individuals with early-onset blindness","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228440880_On_the_intelligibility_of_fast_synthesized_speech_for_individuals_with_early-onset_blindness"},{"title":"nvda@nvda.groups.io | Tips for speed reading /listening with screen readers","url":"https://nvda.groups.io/g/nvda/topic/tips_for_speed_reading/23475120?p=Created,,,20,2,0,0&prev=1"},{"title":"The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users","url":"https://vuink.com/post/fghss-d-dvagreserr-d-dpn/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders-d-dhtml"},{"title":"(PDF) DECtalk and VeriVox: Intelligibility, Likeability, and Rate Preference Differences for Four Listener Groups","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/24195351_DECtalk_and_VeriVox_Intelligibility_Likeability_and_Rate_Preference_Differences_for_Four_Listener_Groups"},{"title":"The voice behind screen readers | Fable","url":"https://makeitfable.com/article/the-voice-behind-screen-readers/"},{"title":"The Great Screen Reader Race: A Review of the Two Leading Screen Readers | Accessworld | American Foundation for the Blind","url":"https://afb.org/aw/2/5/15014"},{"title":"Adaptive methods for controlling the annunciation rate of synthesized speech","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/5749071"},{"title":"Screen Readers | American Foundation for the Blind","url":"https://afb.org/blindness-and-low-vision/using-technology/assistive-technology-products/screen-readers"}]

I found search results related to your query about screen reader users, synthesizer preferences, and speech rates. Here's what the research shows:

## Synthesizer Preferences

Eloquence is by far the most popular speech synthesizer, as it's rather robotic sounding but has just enough human qualities to be pleasant to the ear, as well as understood at a high rate of speech. Eloquence is, in one sampling of synthesizers, the easiest to understand at fast speeds, 350, 400 or more words per minute.

## User Preferences for Speed

While sighted users prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible, blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute, whereas the speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute.

## Intelligibility and Familiarity

Speaking rate is the rate at which the synthesizer announces the synthesized text, and is a powerful contributor to synthesizer intelligibility: it is possible to understand even an extremely poor synthesizer if it speaks slowly enough. Additionally, the more familiar a listener is with the synthesized speech, the faster the listener will want that speech to be.

The research indicates that both Eloquence and DECtalk are commonly studied synthesizers in this context, with Eloquence showing particular strength for intelligibility at very high speech rates preferred by experienced blind screen reader users.

Web search results for query: "time-compressed speech intelligibility content-aware variable rate compression preserve consonant transitions phoneme"

Links: [{"title":"Enhancing speech intelligibility using variable-rate time-scale modification","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7065485"},{"title":"Effects of Compression on Speech Acoustics, Intelligibility, and Sound Quality - Pamela E. Souza, 2002","url":"https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/108471380200600402"},{"title":"The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"System for improving speech quality and intelligibility with bandwidth compression/expansion","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7813931"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed speech: the effect of uniform versus non-uniform time-compression algorithms - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24606289/"},{"title":"Techniques, Perception, and Applications of Time-Compressed Speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/2885840_Techniques_Perception_and_Applications_of_Time-Compressed_Speech"}]

I found several relevant research results on your search topic. Here's what the sources discuss:

## Variable-Rate Time-Scale Modification

A key approach to enhancing speech intelligibility uses variable rate time-scale modification, incorporating spectral enhancements and variable-rate time-scaling procedures to improve the salience of initial consonants, particularly the perceptually important formant transitions.

## Preserving Consonant Information

Emphasis can be transferred from the dominating vowel to the preceding consonant through adaptation of the phoneme timing structure. Time domain characteristics can be modified such that steady-state and non-steady-state parts of speech signals are oppositely modified.

## Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Compression

A uniform and a non-uniform algorithm were used to compress sentences at different speech rates, with the non-uniform algorithm exhibiting greater deviations from targeted time compression as well as greater changes of phoneme duration, spectra, and modulation spectra.

## Phoneme-Dependent Effects

The effects of compression are phoneme-dependent, with the greatest alteration for voiceless fricatives and affricates and minimal effects for nasals and glides.

The research suggests that content-aware compression strategies that selectively modify steady-state versus transitional phonetic elements can better preserve the acoustic cues important for intelligibility, particularly the consonant-vowel transitions.

content.text:
I have strong coverage across both assigned angles. The most directly relevant and authoritative results have emerged. Let me consolidate the top findings.

The standout sources for my angle (engines-config + measurement):

**Angle 5 (engines/config):**
- The "State of Modern AI TTS for Screen Reader Users" article (practitioner community) is highly relevant — directly compares neural vs formant for speed.
- The fast synthesized speech intelligibility study (peer-reviewed) quantifies WPM preferences.
- Kokoro/Piper comparison guides.

**Angle 6 (measurement):**
- STMI original paper (Elhilali) — the canonical spectro-temporal modulation index reference.
- STOI / ESTOI papers.
- "Rethinking MUSHRA" + "Crowdsourcing MUSHRA in the Age of Generative Speech" (Interspeech 2025) — modern TTS-specific listening test protocols.
- Time-compressed synthetic speech intelligibility (compression method + speaking style) — bridges measurement and DSP.

results.title: A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility (Elhilali, Chi & Shamma, Speech Communication 2003)
results.snippet: Canonical peer-reviewed source defining the STMI metric named in angle 6. STMI elaborates on the STI by incorporating joint (inseparable) spectro-temporal modulation dimensions, integrating filtered spectrograms across the acoustic-frequency axis to capture temporal energy fluctuation per modulation channel. This is the authoritative reference for an objective metric that is sensitive to exactly the spectro-temporal cues degraded by time-compression/speed-up, making it ideal for quantifying whether a fast-but-clear voice preserves intelligibility cues. Direct PDF from the Johns Hopkins LCAP lab.

results.title: Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style (Speech Communication)
results.snippet: Directly tests the angle-6/angle-3 crux: it measures how synthetic-speech intelligibility at high speed depends on (a) the compression method (uniform vs non-uniform time-scale modification) and (b) the speaking style of the source voice (e.g., clear/hyperarticulated). This is the closest peer-reviewed evidence to the user's central question of whether a clear-speech source or smarter compression buys measurable intelligibility gains over naive time-compression, and it anchors which conditions to A/B in a listening test.

results.title: The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (2026 practitioner write-up)
results.snippet: Community/practitioner knowledge from the blind speed-listening world directly supporting angles 2 and 5. States sighted users prefer natural/human-like voices while blind power users prefer fast, clear, predictable, efficient voices, explaining the preference for robotic formant synths (Eloquence/DECtalk) understandable at 800-900 WPM vs ~200-250 WPM normal speech. Useful for justifying a separate aggressively speed-optimized track and for the head-to-head neural-vs-formant framing.

results.title: Crowdsourcing MUSHRA Tests in the Age of Generative Speech Technologies (Interspeech 2025)
results.snippet: Modern, TTS-specific MUSHRA protocol guidance for angle 6's subjective-evaluation requirement. Covers how to run reliable MUSHRA-style listening tests (hidden reference, anchors, 0-100 scale, ~10s stimuli, post-screening rule excluding raters who score the hidden reference <90 on >15% of items) adapted for generative speech and crowdsourcing. This is the practical recipe for deciding whether a fine-tune cleared a 'significant gain' bar before committing effort.

results.title: Rethinking MUSHRA: Addressing Modern Challenges in Text-to-Speech Evaluation (arXiv 2024)
results.snippet: Complements the Interspeech MUSHRA paper for angle 6 by critiquing classic MUSHRA when applied to high-quality modern TTS and proposing fixes (e.g., reference/anchor handling when synthetic samples rival natural speech). Relevant for designing an A/B/MUSHRA protocol that can still discriminate small intelligibility differences between a baseline Kokoro voice, a DSP-enhanced track, and a fine-tuned fast voice.

results.title: On the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech for individuals with early-onset blindness
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed quantification underpinning angles 2 and 5: measures intelligibility of synthesized speech at elevated rates for blind listeners, supporting the claim that trained power listeners comprehend speech far above normal rates and that rate is a dominant intelligibility lever. Useful as a citation for setting target syllable/word rates and for justifying the optional power-listener track plus the listening-test rate conditions to evaluate.

content.text: I've completed the research and returned the top 6 results via StructuredOutput, ranked by relevance to the original question across angles 5 (engines/config) and 6 (measurement).
